title: The State v Small, Warren
court: High Court of Justice, Criminal Division, Port of Spain
defendants: Warren Small aka Warren "Quincy" Small aka "Blacks"

individuals.name: Darrie Simon
individuals.notes: Shot and killed at Acono Junction, Maracas, St Joseph on 3 March 2020; son of eyewitness Ms Joseph Tinto

individuals.name: Sharlene Ramkissoon
individuals.notes: Killed in the same shooting on 3 March 2020

individuals.name: Ms Joseph Tinto
individuals.notes: Key recognition witness; minimart owner and mother of deceased Darrie Simon; recognition evidence found insufficient

individuals.name: Ms Celine Rebeiro
individuals.notes: Daughter of Sharlene Ramkissoon; formal admission; did not identify the accused

criminal_individuals.name: Warren Small
criminal_individuals.notes: Aka Quincy/Blacks. Charged over a 3 March 2020 shooting at Acono Junction, Maracas, St Joseph. Found Not Guilty on all four counts; identification depended substantially on recognition evidence the Court found of insufficient quality.

social_headline: Acono Junction double-murder accused walks free as judge rejects 'masked face' ID
social_post: Warren Small, known as Quincy or Blacks, has been found Not Guilty of the March 2020 murders of Darrie Simon and Sharlene Ramkissoon at Acono Junction, Maracas. The State's case rested mainly on one eyewitness, the minimart owner who was also Simon's mother. She said she recognised Small when a bandana slipped from a gunman's face. Justice Nalini Singh ruled the recognition was too brief, partly obscured by a glass display case, made during a chaotic shooting, and first reported two years later. Firearm and ammunition charges fell with the murder counts. All four counts: Not Guilty. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Recognition evidence of a known person still requires special caution where the viewing was brief, partly obscured or made in chaotic conditions
Prior familiarity with an accused does not by itself cure weaknesses in the observation
Investigative gaps (no ID parade, no timely line-of-sight reconstruction) can deprive the court of support needed to be sure of identity
Delay in giving a first statement weakens the ability to test consistency of an identification
outcome: Accused found Not Guilty on all counts (two murders, possession of firearm and ammunition). Identity not proven to the criminal standard; recognition evidence held to be of insufficient quality.
osint_notes: Purely criminal matter (double murder / firearms) with no commercial entities, financial figures or property interests. Tracked for crime intelligence: firearms homicide, acquittal on identification grounds.

social_headline: Tunapuna family feud: judge upholds dad's deed of gift to daughter over son's estate claim
social_post: A Tunapuna estate fight has ended with the High Court upholding a father's gift of the family home to his daughter. Cyril Holder transferred No. 26 El Dorado Road to Mary Marshall by a 2021 Deed of Gift. His son Garland, later appointed his Committee and then estate representative, challenged it as undue influence and claimed Cyril lacked mental capacity, also alleging she pocketed $210,000 in pension money. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC found the deed was Cyril's free and independent will, the capacity evidence fell short, and the pension allegation was untrue - records showed about $91,000 in cheques over nearly three years. Claim dismissed; the property stays out of the estate. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The presumption of undue influence in a gift between family members can be rebutted with evidence the donor acted of free and independent will
A medical diagnosis made long after a transaction, without expert evidence on the disease's trajectory, may fail to prove lack of capacity at the relevant date
Unproven allegations of financial misappropriation can undermine a claimant's credibility and costs position
outcome: Claim dismissed. Deed of Gift dated 21 April 2021 declared lawful and valid; Property excluded from Cyril Holder's estate. No account or repayment of pension monies ordered. Costs reserved; further submissions due 12 June 2026.
osint_notes: Real-property transfer with named address, deed numbers and pension figures; estate/family dispute. No commercial entities but tangible property and financial data of OSINT value.

social_headline: State to pay remand prisoner $290k after officers beat him in a sewage drain
social_post: The High Court has ordered the State to pay remand prisoner Sayyid Obi Albert after prison officers beat him with batons in a filth-filled slip drain at the Port of Spain Remand Prison in February 2016, fracturing his shin. The State argued the force was lawful self-defence after Albert allegedly stabbed an inmate, but Justice Robin Mohammed found the beating unlawful and excessive. Albert, on remand for murder since 2011, was awarded $180,000 general and aggravated damages, $50,000 exemplary damages, plus interest, costs and expert fees - over $290,000 in total. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The State is vicariously liable for unlawful and excessive force used by prison officers under the State Liability and Proceedings Act
Exemplary damages punish and deter but generally do not attract pre-judgment interest, unlike compensatory and aggravated damages
Expert fees are assessed separately and excluded from the prescribed-costs value of a claim
outcome: Judgment for the claimant. State ordered to pay TT$180,000 general/aggravated damages, TT$50,000 exemplary damages, TT$46,368.49 pre-judgment interest, TT$49,136.85 prescribed costs and TT$12,000 expert fees.

social_headline: Petit Valley family land war: Corridon relatives ordered off the property in 60 days
social_post: A bitter family land dispute in Petit Valley has been settled in court. Keola Corridon and four co-claimants sued paternal relatives Ashford Corridon and five others over a 4,633 sq ft portion of the La Puerta Estate on Sparrow Drive, Diego Martin, seeking possession and trespass damages. The defendants counterclaimed an equitable interest. Justice Robin Mohammed ruled the claimants the outright winners: they get possession, $30,000 in trespass damages plus interest, and the relatives must vacate within 60 days. The counterclaim was dismissed with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Documentary title under a registered deed generally prevails over relatives' informal claims of equitable interest
A counterclaim is a claim in its own right and carries its own costs if it fails
Non-monetary claims are valued at $50,000 for prescribed-costs purposes (CPR 67.5(2)(c))
outcome: Judgment for the claimants. Possession granted; defendants to deliver up within 60 days and pay $30,000 trespass damages plus $5,597.26 interest and $14,000 costs on each of the claim and counterclaim. Counterclaim dismissed.

related_litigation: High Court CV2023-00054 (Mohan)
High Court CV2023-01012 (Ransome)
social_headline: Court of Appeal: TTPS broke the law posting charged officers' mugshots on Facebook
social_post: The Court of Appeal has ruled that the Trinidad and Tobago Police Service acted unlawfully when it published the mugshots of two of its own officers on Facebook after they were charged with misbehaviour in public office over a bribery accusation. PCs Rishi Mohan and Ronald Ransome challenged the postings. The Appeal Court, led by Justice Rajkumar with CJ Boodoosingh and Justice Mark Mohammed, held the publication breached their rights to privacy and the presumption of innocence and was ultra vires the Police Service Act. Each officer was awarded $10,000; the TTPS was ordered to take the mugshots down. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Police powers to photograph arrested persons under s.50 of the Police Service Act are for identification, not public dissemination of mugshots
Publishing the mugshot of a charged-but-untried person can breach the constitutional right to privacy and the presumption of innocence
A declaration of unlawfulness plus modest damages may suffice as vindication without a separate vindicatory award
outcome: Mohan's appeal-related award reduced to $10,000 (vindicatory damages and equality declaration set aside). Ransome's appeal allowed: publication declared unconstitutional and ultra vires the Police Service Act; $10,000 damages. TTPS ordered to remove the mugshots.
osint_notes: Constitutional/privacy matter involving two police officers charged with bribery/misbehaviour in public office. No commercial entities or property. Crime tracking: police corruption charges.

title: Maloney v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
court: Court of Appeal, Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Timothy Maloney
individuals.notes: Former Trinidad and Tobago Defence Force/Regiment sergeant; alleged unconstitutional denial of promotion and discharge procedures; appeal dismissed

individuals.name: Lt Col Patrick Gomez
individuals.notes: Said to have recommended Maloney for promotion; held not to be the decision-maker

related_litigation: High Court CV2021-01544 (dismissed by Margaret Mohammed J)
social_headline: Soldier who lost out on promotion loses constitutional appeal against the Defence Force
social_post: The Court of Appeal has dismissed a constitutional claim by former Defence Force soldier Timothy Maloney, who argued he was unfairly denied promotion and forced into retirement in 2014. Maloney said he was promised a recommendation and even attended a promotion ceremony, but the post never materialised. The court, led by Chief Justice Ronnie Boodoosingh, found he had pending disciplinary charges and a finding of guilt that he accepted in lieu of a court martial, so he was not in good standing for promotion. With no comparator and no clear binding promise, his claims of unequal treatment and breached legitimate expectation failed. Appeal dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A legitimate expectation requires a clear, unambiguous promise from someone empowered to make it
An employee not in good standing due to pending or proven disciplinary charges cannot expect promotion
Equality-of-treatment claims require evidence of a real or hypothetical comparator treated differently
outcome: Appeal dismissed; trial judge's dismissal and costs order affirmed. Parties to be heard on costs of the appeal.
osint_notes: Constitutional/employment matter concerning a Defence Force promotion dispute. No commercial entities, property or financial awards; no criminal content beyond an internal military disciplinary finding.

social_headline: $20k surplus from a credit union slip-up sparks court fight over sale of 'DV8' business
social_post: A dispute over the sale of a Trinidad business called DV8 has reached the High Court. Buyer Quinten Questel agreed to purchase the going concern from Shem Thomas for $135,000 in June 2019, paying a $20,000 deposit. A credit union error then sent Thomas the full $135,000 loan on top of the deposit, leaving a $20,000 surplus Questel says was never returned - so he sued for $155,000. In this ruling, Justice Robin Mohammed allowed only part of Questel's proposed Reply to the defence, striking out most of it as an attempt to re-plead the case. The matter heads to a further case management conference. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A Reply serves only to address genuinely new matters raised in a defence, not to re-plead or add evidence
Facts that could and should have been pleaded in the Statement of Case cannot be smuggled in via a Reply
Pleading that a statement was made and relied upon is not barred as hearsay at the pleading stage
outcome: Procedural ruling: claimant granted permission to file a Reply limited to paragraphs 1, 3, 8 and 9; paragraphs 2, 4, 5, 6 and 7 struck out. No order as to costs; further CMC set for 29 June 2026.
osint_notes: Commercial dispute over the sale of a named business (DV8) with specific purchase figures and a credit union involvement. Business and financial data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

social_headline: Point Fortin couple lose appeal blaming borough's drain works for cracking their home
social_post: A Point Fortin couple have lost their appeal against the Borough Corporation over damage to their Hillcrest Drive home. Leslie Ann Rahim-Peters and Larry Eddie Peters said a 2015 drainage excavation next to their northern boundary caused their retaining wall to collapse and cracked the foundation, walls and floor of their house. The Borough's contractor shored up the wall and later rebuilt it, arguing the cracks pre-dated the works. The Court of Appeal, with Justice Rahim writing, upheld the trial judge: the owners bore the burden of proving causation and led no geotechnical evidence, so the claim failed. Their appeal on costs also fell. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: In a negligence claim for property damage the claimant bears the burden of proving causation
Where soil movement and structural damage are alleged, geotechnical/expert evidence is essential to discharge that burden
Diminution in value (pre- vs post-incident valuations) can set the claim value for prescribed-costs purposes
outcome: Substantive appeal and appeal on costs dismissed; trial judge's dismissal and costs order ($48,065 against appellants) affirmed.

social_headline: Brothers' fight over late father's will: judge strikes out swathes of 'opinion' evidence
social_post: A High Court estate battle between brothers Brian and Gary Howard over their late father Cecil's will and power of attorney has produced a ruling cleaning up the evidence before trial. Brian claims Cecil lacked capacity and that Gary used undue influence to become the major beneficiary, controlling the father's properties - including a pub, an apartment and a Woodbrook home - and bank accounts. Justice Margaret Mohammed struck out two entire witness statements and large portions of others, ruling that much of the material was irrelevant, speculative, hearsay, unqualified expert opinion, or a departure from the pleaded case. The substantive dispute continues. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Lay witnesses cannot give expert opinion on a testator's mental capacity
Witness statements must stay within the pleaded case and avoid speculation, conclusions and hearsay
Striking out improper evidence pre-trial narrows the issues the court must decide
outcome: Evidential ruling: witness statements of Mario Howard-Walker and Dzuel Gittens struck out entirely; numerous paragraphs of the statements of Brian Howard, Joan Gittens, Gazelle Robertson, Mary Howard and Gary Howard struck out for irrelevance, opinion/expert evidence, hearsay or departure from the pleaded case.
osint_notes: Procedural evidential ruling in a family estate/undue-influence dispute. Property interests (pub, apartment, Woodbrook home) referenced but without addresses or figures; no criminal charges. Limited concrete OSINT data.

social_headline: Father found guilty of sexually abusing his 11-year-old daughter in judge-alone trial
social_post: A father has been found guilty of sexually abusing his young daughter in a judge-alone trial before Justice Lisa Ramsumair-Hinds. The accused, whose name is withheld to protect the child, faced five counts of sexual offences said to have occurred when the girl was 11, in late 2019 and mid-2020. The judge found the child's account credible beyond reasonable doubt, including her explanation for delaying her complaint - that she had promised not to tell and feared getting her father in trouble. He was convicted of three counts of sexual touching of a child and one count of sexual penetration of a child. Sentencing follows. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Delay in a child complaining of sexual abuse does not automatically undermine credibility, especially where the child fears consequences for a parent
A child's evidence can be sufficient to convict beyond reasonable doubt where it is compelling and age-consistent
Whether touching is 'sexual' is assessed by reference to the acts and all surrounding circumstances
outcome: Guilty on four counts: three counts of sexual touching of a child (Counts 1, 2, 4) and one count of sexual penetration of a child (Count 5).

social_headline: Political leader Kirk Waithe must face creative team's $67k campaign-fee claim
social_post: A High Court judge has refused to throw out a claim by three creatives who say political leader Kirk Waithe never fully paid them for running his party's 2020 election advertising. Nicole Martin, Quincy Ross and Richard Lewis say they were hired as a team by Waithe - leader of the Nationwide Organisation of We the People (NOW) - and are still owed $67,250 after only partial cash and cheque payments. Waithe argued the suit was an abuse of process and belonged in the Petty Civil Court. Justice Robin Mohammed disagreed, found a serious issue to be tried, dismissed the strike-out, ordered Waithe to pay costs and gave him 28 days to file a defence. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A claim is not an abuse of process merely because an earlier identical claim was withdrawn, where the first claim was never struck out or adjudicated
A jurisdiction challenge must be filed within the time for filing a defence (28 days), or the defendant is treated as accepting jurisdiction
Multiple claimants under a single alleged contract may aggregate their claims to meet the High Court threshold
outcome: Defendant's application to strike out for abuse of process / lack of jurisdiction dismissed. Defendant treated as accepting jurisdiction; granted 28 days to file a defence and ordered to pay the claimants' costs of the application.

social_headline: New mum's hospital-burn negligence case stumbles as amended claim is struck out
social_post: A medical negligence claim against Gulf View Medical Centre has hit a procedural wall. New mother Renata Ramroop-Deoraj alleges that after giving birth in 2016 she was given a Sitz bath with water too hot to feel under epidural anaesthesia, leaving her with first-to-third degree burns to 2% of her body. The hospital says it absorbed $132,188 in her treatment costs. In this ruling, Justice Robin Mohammed struck out her Amended Statement of Case because she filed it after the first case management conference without the court's permission. She must pay the hospital's costs of the application; the underlying negligence claim continues on the original pleadings. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A statement of case may be amended without permission only before the first case management conference; once the CMC has commenced, permission is required even with the other side's consent
A CMC is a stage in proceedings, not a single hearing - active case management (e.g. granting consent directions) marks its commencement
After the first CMC, a party must show a good explanation and act promptly to be allowed to amend
outcome: Defendant's application succeeds: claimant's Amended Statement of Case struck out under CPR 26.2(1)(a) and (b). Claimant to pay the costs of the application; matter set for a further CMC on 8 June 2026.
osint_notes: Medical negligence claim against a named private hospital (Gulf View Medical Centre Ltd) with its Managing Director identified and a medical-cost figure. Corporate and financial data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

social_headline: St James neighbours lose battle to fence off shared steps to a Nile Street home
social_post: A long-running St James boundary dispute has been decided in the High Court. Dexter Kelly and Gemma Ramdhanie-Goddard sued neighbours Virginia Adams and Abigail Salandy after the pair erected a 55-foot chain-link fence and locked gate in 2017, sealing off the steps that lead from Nile Street up to the family's home at 3A Nile Street. A joint surveyor found the steps sit mostly on the claimants' land. Justice Avason Quinlan-Williams held the claimants had been in lawful possession since before 1962 and the defendants never had exclusive possession for the 16 years needed to claim the steps. The fence must come down; the claimants get $5,000 trespass damages and $10,000 costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: To defeat a lawful possessor by adverse possession, a defendant must prove 16 years of exclusive possession with intention to exclude the owner
Trespass to land in another's possession is actionable without proof of actual damage
A court may reject a surveyor/expert's opinion where it is not founded on evidence or tends to usurp the judge's function
outcome: Judgment for the claimants: defendants must demolish and remove the chain-link fence and gate and not obstruct access to 3A Nile Street; defendants to pay $5,000 nominal trespass damages and $10,000 costs. Claimants' special-damages (rental) claim rejected.

social_headline: Lab tech wins $370k after Ministry of Health forced a transfer 16 years late and cut her pay
social_post: A Medical Laboratory Technician has won $370,000 in constitutional damages from the State. Charmaine Quashie opted in 2007 to transfer from the Ministry of Health to the South West Regional Health Authority, but - through an administrative oversight - she was never told it was approved. In 2023 the Ministry suddenly tried to force the transfer through, then stopped her salary for five months when she refused. Justice Avason Quinlan-Williams found breaches of her rights to due process, equality and enjoyment of property, awarding $200,000 compensatory, $150,000 vindicatory and $20,000 exemplary damages. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Withholding a public servant's salary without a hearing can breach the constitutional right to enjoyment of property
An employer cannot force a transfer 16 years late without notice, consultation or an opportunity to be heard
Exemplary and vindicatory damages are available where state conduct is outrageous and warrants public denunciation
outcome: Judgment for the claimant. Multiple constitutional breaches declared; State ordered to pay $200,000 compensatory, $150,000 vindicatory and $20,000 exemplary damages. Parties to be heard on costs.

social_headline: Arima man's police-beating claim sunk by the one thing he wouldn't change: the date
social_post: A High Court judge has dismissed Dexter Carter's claim that Arima municipal police beat him at his home, even while accepting he probably was assaulted. Carter pleaded the attack happened on 11 March 2021 and named officer Brandon Cumberbatch. But Cumberbatch produced training registers and station-diary records placing him at a workshop in Marabella all that day, and Carter's own medical report was timestamped hours before the alleged incident. Justice Westmin James held the court could only decide the case as pleaded - and Carter never amended the date - so the claim failed despite the evidence of some assault. The defence noted the suit followed drug-trafficking charges Cumberbatch had laid against Carter's brother. Claim dismissed with $14,000 costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Parties are bound by their pleadings; a court can only decide the case as pleaded, not on an unpleaded alternative date
A material fact such as the date of an assault must be amended if the evidence shifts, or a verified alibi can be fatal
Poorly drafted pleadings - omitting causes of action like wrongful arrest or unpleaded injuries (PTSD) - can sink an otherwise arguable claim
outcome: Claim for assault and battery dismissed. Although the court accepted an assault likely occurred, the claimant failed to prove it happened on the pleaded date of 11 March 2021 against the first defendant's verified alibi. Claimant to pay $14,000 costs; 42-day stay of execution.
osint_notes: Civil assault claim against police, dismissed on pleadings. No commercial entities or property. Crime tracking: collateral drug-trafficking charges against the claimant's brother by the defendant officer.

social_headline: Court upholds 7-day emergency detention of alleged 'Resistance Gang' shooter
social_post: The High Court has dismissed a challenge to the detention of Denesh Maharaj, arrested during Trinidad and Tobago's 2025 State of Emergency. Police say they found a Venezuelan-marked pistol, ammunition and cannabis at his home and that intelligence identified him as a main shooter of the Resistance Gang, possibly linked to the disappearance of businessman Imraz Ali. He argued his further seven-day detention under the Emergency Powers Regulations was unlawful. Justice Frank Seepersad held the detention order was measured, reasonable and proportionate given the national-security context, and noted Maharaj had failed to disclose the firearm and drugs found at his home. Claim dismissed with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Courts afford significant deference to executive and police operational decisions on national security, especially during a State of Emergency
A further detention order under EPR reg 13(3) is lawful where an ASP-or-above is satisfied inquiries cannot be completed within 48 hours
An applicant for constitutional/judicial review relief must come with full candour; concealing material facts undermines credibility
outcome: Claim dismissed. The decision to issue the seven-day Further Detention Order under the Emergency Powers Regulations 2025 held lawful, reasonable and proportionate. Claimant to pay the defendants' costs; first defendant's costs certified fit for senior counsel.

The State's case is that after the killings he set fire to a car belonging to the mother of his estranged wife's new partner. An accused who testifies has his character weighed on credibility as well as propensity

social_post: A High Court judge has dismissed a malicious-prosecution claim by Daron Ashton, an estate constable with Allied Security Services who was assigned to the Judiciary's IT Department. Ashton was arrested in June 2014 and charged over laptop bags worth $1,600 that went missing from the building. He sued the State, but his unlawful-arrest and detention claims were abandoned as time-barred. On the surviving malicious-prosecution claim, Justice Karen Reid found that the charging officer, Sgt Bissoon, had reasonable and probable cause and acted without malice. social_headline: Tobago land feud: dead man's son can't enforce 24-year-old order but can still fight for possession
social_post: A long-running Tobago land dispute over the Sherwood Park and Lambeau estates has produced a key ruling. Andre Birchwood, executor of his late father Cassell's estate, sued Marilyn Duke - who built a ten-bedroom apartment complex on the land and agreed to sell another parcel - to recover property his father was promised under a 2000 consent order. Justice Karen Reid struck out the bid to enforce that order as 24 years too late, but let the possession and trust claims go to trial. She also found Duke had not been fully forthright about a new gate and drums blocking the access road, and granted an injunction protecting Birchwood's access pending trial. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A judgment or consent order for conveyance of land must generally be enforced within twelve years or it becomes statute-barred
An executor can sue on a will before probate, but needs a limited grant (ad litem and ad colligenda bona) to obtain final relief
Interim injunctions turn on the balance of justice - which course causes the least irremediable prejudice pending trial
outcome: Claims to enforce the 2000 consent order and for maladministration struck out as statute-barred; claims in possession and for declaratory/trust relief allowed to proceed with amended pleadings. Additional injunction granted restraining the defendants from blocking the claimant's access. Each side bears own costs.

social_post: A Felicity family home dispute has ended with the occupier ordered to leave. The Motilals let their relative Stephan Ramjitsingh and his wife live in their house at No. 108 Cacandee Road as an act of kindness after their child was born. Years later, Ramjitsingh's estate claimed he had an equitable interest, saying he spent up to $445,992 repairing the property and caring for relatives. Justice Joan Charles preferred the owners' account, finding no promise that he could stay for life and no encouragement to spend to his detriment. The counterclaim was dismissed, he was declared a trespasser, and his estate must give vacant possession within 60 days and pay $28,000 costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A claim for equitable interest based on proprietary estoppel requires a clear promise or assurance plus detrimental reliance
Being allowed to occupy a home as an act of kindness does not create an interest in the land
A counterclaimant must support alleged expenditure with reliable receipts, not just bank withdrawals
outcome: Judgment for the claimants; defendant's counterclaim for equitable interest dismissed. Declarations of no interest and of trespass, injunctions and an order for vacant possession within 60 days; defendant to pay $28,000 costs.

Chapman had simply handed over the keys without saying he wasn't the owner. Her father, Hubert Chapman, filed an interpleader claim with the registration card proving title. Justice Robin Mohammed ordered the truck released to the estate, found the judgment creditor bound by an indemnity his former attorney signed, and split the $45,506 in storage fees 50/50 between creditor and debtor. Where a debtor misleads marshals about ownership, storage costs can be apportioned between creditor and debtor

Proprietary estoppel can give an interest to someone who builds on another's land with the owner's encouragement or silent acquiescence

social_headline: Siparia couple win a lot by adverse possession after 22 years of fencing and farming
social_post: A family land dispute in Siparia has handed Anthony Victor and Maria Maraj-Victor ownership of a lot they never held on paper. The registered owners, the Ramsubhaghs, sued for possession, but the Victors had occupied and transformed the lot - backfilling swamp, fencing it, building sheds and rearing chickens - from 1995 to 2017. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC held their open, continuous acts of ownership over more than sixteen years extinguished the paper owners' title under the Real Property Limitation Act, so the Victors get a fee simple interest. However, a separate claim that the land had been promised to their migrated son Ronnie failed, as he never relied on the promise to his own detriment. Each side bears its own costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Possession that begins with permission can become adverse once the occupier's acts go beyond the licence and show an intention to exclude the owner
Sixteen years of open, exclusive possession extinguishes a paper owner's title under the Real Property Limitation Act
Proprietary estoppel fails where the person claiming relied on the promise was not the one to whom it was made, or suffered no real detriment
outcome: Defendants succeed on adverse possession: claimants' title to the disputed lot extinguished and defendants granted a fee simple interest; claim for possession/trespass dismissed. Defendants' counterclaims for proprietary estoppel and constructive trust dismissed. Each party bears own costs.

social_headline: Court strikes down Registrar's online-only company filing as unlawful overreach
social_post: Seven companies in the Galy corporate group have won a judicial review against the Registrar General over the Companies Registry Online System (CROS). Since 2023 the Registry had refused hard-copy filings, forcing everything online and, the companies said, stalling their banking business. Justice Frank Seepersad ruled the Registrar acted ultra vires: the Companies Act still allows in-person filing under section 471, and subordinate regulations cannot abolish that right or add new refusal grounds beyond section 487. He also found the move breached the public's legitimate expectation of a hybrid system and created unreasonable, privacy-sensitive barriers. The mandatory-online decision was quashed and the Registrar ordered to accept hard-copy documents. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Subordinate legislation and administrative policy cannot override or amend the parent Act - only Parliament can
A public body cannot abolish a statutorily-contemplated method of compliance (in-person filing) by regulation or practice
Withdrawing a long-settled service can breach a legitimate expectation unless rational grounds and fair notice are given
outcome: Claim succeeds. Declarations that the Registrar General's refusal of hard-copy post-incorporation filings (mandating CROS) was ultra vires, unlawful and a breach of legitimate expectation; certiorari quashing the mandatory-electronic-filing decision; mandamus compelling acceptance of hard-copy documents; costs to claimants. 42-day stay.
osint_notes: Corporate/judicial-review matter naming seven companies (the Galy group, with banking-related business) and two directors. Useful corporate-network and AML/CFT-context data; no criminal content.

social_headline: Seller who broke a deal to grab a $400k bulldozer back must now pay the buyer
social_post: A contractor has turned the tables on a supplier in a bulldozer dispute. Arion Contractors bought a used CAT D6R XL dozer from Hydraulic Supplies for over $1 million on instalments, then fell behind. The two signed a July 2019 deal: the supplier would hold off suing if the arrears were cleared by October. Instead, just over two weeks later, the supplier took a default judgment, garnished $21,000 from the contractor's accounts and seized and resold the bulldozer for $400,000. Justice Joan Charles found the supplier breached the forbearance agreement and had no right to repossess goods it had already delivered, awarding the buyer $400,000 plus $100,000 damages and setting aside the default judgment. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A creditor who agrees to forbear from enforcement cannot take judgment or execute before the agreed payment date
Under the Sale of Goods Act, an unpaid seller who has delivered the goods loses the right to repossess without a court order
Obtaining a judgment for the price does not by itself revive a right to seize delivered goods
outcome: Judgment for the claimant for breach of the 15 July 2019 agreement. Seller to pay $400,000 for the lost bulldozer plus $100,000 damages and interest at 1.5%; the 31 July 2019 default judgment set aside and the balance owing set off. Defendant to pay prescribed costs.

social_headline: Signal Hill family beats a registered title - decades of living there extinguished the paper owner
social_post: A Tobago land dispute at Signal Hill has ended with long-time occupiers defeating a registered title. Jhodi Skeete, suing for her elderly grandmother who held a 2019 Certificate of Title to half a hectare on Mentor Drive, demanded the Mark family and Andre Phillips demolish their homes and leave. But the defendants showed they and their forebears had occupied and built on the land for well over sixteen years - some over forty - without permission. Justice Joan Charles held that even an indefeasible registered title under the Real Property Act takes effect subject to subsisting adverse-possession rights, so the owner's title to the occupied lots was extinguished. The lots will be excised and transferred to the occupiers, and the owner pays $28,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Title registered under the Real Property Act is indefeasible but still subject to rights subsisting under adverse possession
Failing to cross-examine opposing witnesses or to plead a reply can amount to admitting their evidence of long occupation
Sixteen years of unpermitted, exclusive occupation can extinguish even a registered owner's title to the occupied portion
outcome: Judgment for the defendants on their counterclaim; claim dismissed. Claimant's registered title to Lots 2, 2A and 4 extinguished by adverse possession; declarations of the defendants' entitlement, an order to excise and transfer those lots, and injunctions. Claimant to pay $14,000 costs on the claim and $14,000 on the counterclaim.

social_headline: Widow keeps Trincity home: unregistered transfer couldn't cut the joint tenancy
social_post: The Court of Appeal has restored a Trincity widow's ownership of the family home at No. 2 Ninth Street, Dinsley Gardens. After Jewan Siewnarine died, his daughters Diana and Victoria claimed a half share, relying on an unregistered 2012 transfer he had signed of his interest. A trial judge agreed, but the Appeal Court - led by Justice Pemberton - held that under the Real Property Act an unregistered memorandum of transfer cannot sever a joint tenancy, so his widow Parbatie took the whole property by survivorship. The court did, however, uphold the finding that she committed no fraud in registering as sole owner. The daughters must pay $90,000 in costs across both courts. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Under the Real Property Act, an unregistered memorandum of transfer does not sever a joint tenancy or defeat the right of survivorship
Registration, not mere execution, is what affects title to RPA land
A surviving joint tenant generally takes the whole property on the other's death unless severance was validly effected
outcome: Appeal allowed. Trial findings that the 2012 unregistered transfer severed the joint tenancy and gave the daughters a half share set aside; no-fraud finding upheld. Surviving wife entitled by survivorship. Respondents to pay costs of $54,000 (High Court) and $36,000 (Court of Appeal).

social_headline: Civil court, not Family Court, will hear ex-husband's bid to undo a $1M divorce deal
social_post: A man who agreed to pay his ex-wife $1 million from the sale of their matrimonial home - and to gift a Tabaquite property to her son - is trying to undo the deal, saying he was coerced and misled while unrepresented. The question for Justice Robin Mohammed was which court should hear it. He ruled that once a matrimonial consent order is perfected, the Family Court that made it is functus officio and cannot set it aside; the proper route is a fresh action in the Civil High Court before a different judge, especially as a new party and a Deed of Gift are involved. The case proceeds as a civil claim. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A perfected consent order is a final judgment; the court that made it cannot vary or set it aside (it is functus officio)
Challenging a consent order for fraud, duress or mistake requires a fresh action or an appeal, not an application in the original proceedings
Strike-out is draconian relief that requires a formal application, not bare submissions
outcome: Point in limine dismissed: the Civil High Court has jurisdiction to hear the claim to set aside the matrimonial consent order. The matter proceeds as a civil action in San Fernando; defendants' informal strike-out request refused; costs of the preliminary issue to the claimant.
osint_notes: Matrimonial/jurisdiction dispute involving a $1M settlement, a matrimonial home and a Tabaquite property gift. Property and financial data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

social_headline: Man who said his Marabella home was sold by an impostor loses - he never took the stand
social_post: A claim that a Marabella property was stolen by forgery has failed because the owner never gave evidence. Oriel La Borde, suing through his sister under a power of attorney, alleged that an impostor signed away his home at No. 63 Battoo Boulevard to Raymond and Petra Pennie, who mortgaged it to TTMF for $472,500. But La Borde filed no witness statement, did not attend to be cross-examined, and led no handwriting expert. Justice Joan Charles drew an adverse inference that he had in fact signed the conveyance, found his attorney's evidence unreliable, and held fraud - an inherently improbable allegation requiring cogent proof - was not made out. The claim was dismissed with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Allegations of fraud or forgery require cogent evidence; the more serious the allegation, the stronger the evidence needed on the balance of probabilities
A claimant who fails to testify or be cross-examined risks an adverse inference, especially where the defence raises a prima facie case
A bona fide purchaser for value without notice of fraud is protected under s.21 of the Real Property Limitation Act
outcome: Claim dismissed; judgment for the defendants. The court drew an adverse inference from the claimant's failure to testify and found the forgery/fraud allegation unproven. Claimant to pay the defendants' costs.
osint_notes: Property-fraud claim (dismissed) involving an identified Marabella parcel, a named mortgage lender (TTMF) and mortgage/value figures. Property and lending data of OSINT value; alleged forgery not proven and no charges.

social_headline: Firearms Appeal Board used the wrong test - FLOW executive wins fresh gun-licence hearing
social_post: A FLOW technical-operations director has won a judicial review against the Firearms Appeal Board over the refusal of his gun licence. Robindranath Maharaj applied for a Firearm User's Licence citing personal and family protection - his father was murdered in 2001, his wife survived an attempted kidnapping in 2008, and he collects cash rent and travels for work. The Board dismissed his appeal on the basis there was no 'imminent threat'. Justice Joan Charles held that the Firearms Act test is whether an applicant has a 'good reason' to possess a firearm, not whether a threat is imminent, so the Board erred in law. She ordered a rehearing without the imminence requirement and awarded half costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The statutory test for a firearm licence is 'good reason' for possession plus no danger to public safety, not 'imminence of threat'
A decision-maker should assess an application from the applicant's standpoint, not merely look for objections
An error of law alone (applying the wrong test) does not automatically amount to a breach of natural justice
outcome: Judgment for the claimant. Declaration that the Board's dismissal was an error of law and ultra vires the Firearms Act for applying an 'imminence of threat' test; mandamus ordering a rehearing within 30 days without that requirement. Defendant to pay half the claimant's costs.
osint_notes: Firearms-licence judicial review naming a telecoms employer (FLOW/Columbus Communications), a security firm (Fortress) and rental income. Background includes an unsolved 2001 murder and 2008 attempted kidnapping (the claimant's family, no charges). Corporate/property data of OSINT value.

social_headline: Worker who sued his union for a 'lost' appeal loses - most claims were years too late
social_post: A former Ministry of Health watchman has lost his case against the National Union of Government and Federated Workers. Kendel Romeo accused the union of poor representation in his long-running trade dispute and of refusing to appeal the Industrial Court's 2019 ruling, which had found he abandoned his job and was a 'dishonest witness'. Justice Westmin James held most of his complaints, dating back to 2005-2016, were statute-barred under the four-year limitation period. On the surviving issues, the union's discretionary rules did not require it to fund an appeal it reasonably thought hopeless, and Romeo was free to hire his own lawyer - which he never did. The claim was dismissed with $14,000 costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Contract claims must be brought within four years of each breach; a series of discrete failures is not a single 'continuing breach'
A union's objects clause is aspirational and does not create enforceable contractual rights to fund every appeal
Discretionary legal-assistance rules are not engaged without the required internal recommendation and may exclude members guilty of wilful neglect of duty
outcome: Claim dismissed. Most allegations statute-barred; on the remaining post-2017 conduct, no breach of contract or fiduciary duty established. Claimant to pay $14,000 prescribed costs; 28-day stay of execution.

title: The State v Figaro, Sterlyn
court: High Court of Justice, Criminal Division, Port of Spain

criminal_individuals.name: Sterlyn Figaro
criminal_individuals.notes: Faces a seven-count indictment alleging sexual and related offences against two male children (five counts re one child from 2017; two counts re another from 2019). Applied to quash the indictment over defective witness statements; application dismissed and committal upheld, so the case proceeds to trial.

related_litigation: The State v Goodridge CR-HC-POS-165-2021-1 (relied on regarding statement admissibility)
social_headline: Judge refuses to quash child-sex indictment over a missing 'adult of his choice' phrase
social_post: A man facing a seven-count indictment alleging sexual and related offences against two boys has failed to have the case thrown out on a technicality. Sterlyn Figaro argued the child complainants' statements were inadmissible because they did not expressly say an adult 'of his choice' was present when they were taken, as the Preliminary Inquiry Act requires. Justice G. Gonzales took a purposive approach, holding the words 'of his choice' were included inadvertently and would produce an absurd result for young children who cannot meaningfully choose; with a parent present and the other formalities met, the statements were properly admitted. Even setting aside the investigator's defective statement, a prima facie case remained. The motion to quash was dismissed and the case proceeds. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Courts may read words out of a statute where a literal reading produces an absurd result that defeats Parliament's intent
A child witness statement is not inadmissible merely for omitting the phrase 'adult of his choice' where a parent was present and other formalities are met
A statement that does not show whether the witness swore or affirmed before a Justice of the Peace is fundamentally defective and inadmissible
outcome: Motion to quash the indictment dismissed; committal held valid. Child witnesses' statements ruled admissible (words 'of his choice' read out as inadvertent); the investigator's further statement excluded as defective, but a prima facie case remained. Case proceeds to trial.
osint_notes: Pre-trial criminal ruling in a child sexual-offences/kidnapping prosecution. No commercial entities, property or financial content. Tracked for crime intelligence (sexual offences against minors, kidnapping).

related_litigation: High Court CV2020-00858
social_headline: Police can't keep fingerprints of people arrested but never charged forever, court rules
social_post: In a privacy victory, the Court of Appeal has ruled that police indefinitely retaining the fingerprint impressions and data of people who are arrested but never charged is unconstitutional. Keston Felix brought the challenge under section 4(c) of the Constitution. While he lost the main plank of his appeal (about exonerated persons), the court unanimously held the broader retention regime in the Police Service Act breached the right to private life and, by majority, read the Act down to limit how long such data can be kept. On costs, Justice Bereaux held that because Felix lost the main issue but still won the 'event' - vindicating his privacy right - he gets one-third of the appeal costs, assessed at $27,000. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Indefinite police retention of fingerprint data of people never charged can breach the constitutional right to private life
Costs follow the 'event'; a party who wins overall but loses a major issue may have costs reduced by a percentage
Appeal-court costs are assessed independently of High Court costs where prescribed costs do not apply
outcome: Ruling on costs following the 6 March 2026 judgment. Appellant, though losing the main appeal issue, vindicated his right to private life; awarded one-third of the costs of the appeal, assessed at $27,000.
osint_notes: Constitutional/privacy ruling on police fingerprint-data retention. No commercial entities, property or criminal charges; a single costs figure.

social_headline: Sister's later will exposed as a forgery - even her own expert agreed
social_post: A San Juan inheritance fight has ended with a will being declared a forgery. After Malcolm Jones died in 2015, one daughter, Anastacia Jones-Pierre, tried to probate a 2001 will that cut her sister Agnita's share, claiming she found it in their father's safety deposit box at First Citizens Bank. Agnita challenged it, and the claimant's forensic document examiner concluded the signature wasn't their father's. Tellingly, the defendant's own commissioned expert reached the same conclusion - but she never called him to testify. Justice Robin Mohammed declared the 2001 will a forgery and invalid, upheld the earlier 1995 will, and set aside the probate application. The defendant must now probate the 1995 will and pay assessed costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The party propounding a will must prove due execution and that the testator knew and approved its contents
A witness who admits he did not see the testator sign defeats due execution, no matter what his statement said
Failing to call your own expert whose report undermines your case invites an adverse inference
outcome: The 2001 will declared invalid (forged signature; not duly executed); the 1995 will pronounced valid in solemn form. The 2017 probate application set aside; defendant directed to probate the 1995 will within 60 days or be discharged as executrix. Claimant awarded assessed costs in lieu of prescribed costs.
osint_notes: Probate/forgery dispute with an identified estate property (No. 14 Francis Road, San Juan) and a named bank (First Citizens). A forged signature was found but no criminal charges. Estate/property data of OSINT value.

social_headline: Widow and children lose Pleasantville family home - they occupied with permission, not as owners
social_post: A San Fernando family home battle has gone to the surviving siblings. After Hecliff Haneiph was deported from the US in 1991, his eight siblings let him and his then-pregnant partner Narda live in the family's leasehold house at No. 70 Cedar Drive, Pleasantville, where he ran his tailoring business for 30 years. After Hecliff died in 2022, his widow and children claimed the home by adverse possession. Justice Joan Charles held they had always occupied with Hecliff's permission - and Hecliff himself acknowledged his siblings were co-owners - so there was no adverse possession. The siblings, who renewed the lease by survivorship, were declared entitled to possession; the defendants must leave within three months and pay $30,000 trespass damages plus costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Possession with the consent of an owner cannot be adverse, so it does not extinguish the owner's title
One co-owner's possession is treated as possession on behalf of all co-owners unless clear adverse intention is shown
Courts are reluctant to let occupiers turn an act of family kindness into ownership by estoppel or limitation
outcome: Judgment for the claimants. Defendants declared to be unlawfully in occupation and ordered to deliver up possession of No. 70 Cedar Drive within 3 months, pay $30,000 trespass damages and $14,000 costs; their adverse-possession counterclaim dismissed with a further $14,000 costs.

social_headline: Judge strikes out TTMF foreclosure, calling the mortgage deed 'invalid... perhaps fraudulent'
social_post: A foreclosure action by the Trinidad and Tobago Mortgage Finance Company has been thrown out, with the High Court branding the underlying mortgage deed invalid and 'perhaps fraudulent'. TTMF sued Annette Young for over $1.2 million and possession of her Arima home at The Crossings, Santa Rosa West. But the 2017 deed of mortgage had been created by attaching the old 2010 signature pages to an 'updated' deed, and TTMF's senior counsel ultimately conceded it was invalid. When TTMF tried to expand its claim with new causes of action without amending the claim form, Justice Carol Gobin struck the whole action out as an abuse of process and ordered TTMF to pay the borrower's costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A claim form, not the statement of case, defines the reliefs sought; a statement of case cannot smuggle in new causes of action without a proper amendment
The overriding objective does not let a court ignore plain rules of procedure
Prosecuting a claim on a deed known to be invalid or improperly produced can itself be an abuse of process
outcome: The mortgagee action struck out as an abuse of process. The expanded reliefs (enforceability of the 2009 loan agreement, equitable mortgage, restitution, the larger $1,363,534.84 sum) struck out; the deed of mortgage held invalid/fraudulent. social_headline: State whistle-blower barrister loses bid to make T&T pay for his corruption fallout
social_post: The High Court has dismissed a damages claim by English barrister Vincent Nelson KC against the Trinidad and Tobago Government. In 2017 Nelson struck a deal with then-AG Faris Al Rawi to give a notarised statement exposing a legal-fee kickback scheme allegedly benefiting former Attorney General Anand Ramlogan and attorney Gerald Ramdeen. Nelson later pleaded guilty himself and was fined $2.25 million. He sued, claiming the State breached confidentiality (by sharing his statement with the UK's National Crime Agency) and failed to recommend he not be prosecuted. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC found one breach but held his losses flowed from his own conviction and reputation, not the breach, and fell outside the agreement's indemnity. Claim dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Damages for breach of contract must be caused by the breach, not by independent factors such as the claimant's own conviction
An indemnity is construed strictly; it covers only the losses within its scope
Courts are reluctant to void freely-made agreements on public-policy grounds, especially where the State contracts to assist prosecuting crime
outcome: Claim dismissed. Although the Government breached the confidentiality clause, the claimant's losses (loss of practice, earnings, insurance discount) flowed from his own prosecution, guilty plea and conviction rather than the breach, and fell outside the indemnity in clause 5.
osint_notes: High-profile public-corruption matter naming a former Attorney General (Anand Ramlogan), an attorney (Gerald Ramdeen), a sitting/former minister (Stuart Young), ex-AG Faris Al Rawi and a convicted English KC (Vincent Nelson). Concerns a government legal-fee kickback scheme; strong crime-intelligence and political-network value.

social_headline: Daughter's 18-year-late challenge to a 'stranger's' will over 25 acres fails
social_post: A daughter's bid to overturn her late mother's will and recover 25 acres of land has failed because she waited too long with too little evidence. Veronica De Gale died in 2001 leaving a 1983 will favouring her children and a disputed 2000 will that instead left her 15-acre and 10-acre parcels to two men she allegedly didn't know. Her daughter Elizabeth Pirtam claimed the 2000 will was a forgery and the signature wasn't her mother's. But Justice Joan Charles found the handwriting expert relied on just four sample signatures - too few to prove forgery - and Pirtam had sat on her rights for 18 years while executors and witnesses died. The claim was dismissed with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Allegations of fraud or forgery in a will must be distinctly pleaded and proved with cogent evidence
Handwriting comparisons based on too few sample signatures are unlikely to establish forgery on the balance of probabilities
Long delay in challenging a probated will weakens a claim, especially once witnesses and executors have died
outcome: Claim dismissed. The court declined to revoke the 2000 will or set aside the consequential land transfers; forgery and lack of testamentary capacity were not proven and the 18-year delay counted against the claimant. Claimant to pay the defendants' costs.

social_headline: Anglican Diocese evicts long-time Arima tenant after lease talks stalled on a back-dated rent
social_post: The Anglican Church has won possession of its commercial property at No. 6 Farfan Street, Arima, which the Ramcharan family had occupied under lease since 1947. After the 25-year lease expired in 2018, the parties negotiated a new lease at $190,000 a year but never agreed when the higher rent would start - the tenants refused to backdate it to 2018. Meanwhile they kept paying the old $6,667 rent, which the Diocese returned. Justice Westmin James held there was only a month-to-month tenancy, validly ended by a Notice to Quit, and that tendering rejected rent cannot force a tenancy on a landlord. The estate must vacate by July 2026 and pay $320,888 in mesne profits plus costs; its counterclaim for a new lease was dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A binding agreement for a lease requires unqualified agreement on all essential terms, including the commencement date
A tenant cannot revive or impose a tenancy by paying rent the landlord actively rejects and returns
After a valid Notice to Quit, a holding-over occupier becomes a trespasser liable for mesne profits at market rate
outcome: Judgment for the claimant. Month-to-month tenancy validly terminated by Notice to Quit; defendant to deliver up possession by 9 July 2026 and pay mesne profits of $320,888.84 plus interest and $54,266.93 costs. Counterclaim for a new 25-year lease/estoppel dismissed with $14,000 costs.

social_headline: Insurer COLFIRE shares blame after seven-month delay sending an adjuster to a crash
social_post: A Tobago motor-accident case has spread the blame three ways. Keith Louis was overtaking a stopped car on Milford Road when Melissa Louis pulled out of a minor street and hit him. Justice Joan Charles found both drivers careless, apportioning 70% of the fault to Melissa Louis and 30% to Keith. But the judge also faulted insurer COLFIRE, which took until August 2020 - some seven months after the January crash - to send an adjuster, leaving the claimant's 1997 Lexus stuck at the repair shop racking up storage and rental costs. He was awarded about $90,000 in total: reduced repair costs, storage fees and three months' car rental. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Overtaking near a T-junction without slowing, and emerging from a minor road without stopping, can both amount to negligence (here split 70/30)
Damages for an older vehicle are limited; courts may discount the cost of new parts and apply the liability split
An insurer that delays inspecting a vehicle can be liable for the storage and loss-of-use costs that accrue
outcome: Liability apportioned 70% to the first defendant and 30% to the claimant. Defendants ordered to pay $90,410.70 total special damages (repairs $50,248.20, storage $21,262.50, three months' rental $18,900), plus prescribed costs and 3% interest.

social_headline: Court of Appeal: stop naming magistrates as parties when their rulings are challenged
social_post: A drunk-driving defendant who tried to overturn a magistrate's decision to adjourn his trial has lost his appeal - but the case produced an important reform. Stefan Hearn argued Magistrate Rehana Ali wrongly adjourned his DUI trial when the prosecution's witnesses didn't show up. The Court of Appeal, led by Justice Bereaux, held she had properly weighed the factors, including the seriousness of drunk driving and the public interest, and judicial review is not an appeal on the merits. The court also ruled that the long-standing practice of naming magistrates as parties and cross-examining them on affidavits is wrong and must stop, to protect judicial impartiality. Such challenges should be defended by the prosecution, not the judge. Appeal dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Judicial review examines the decision-making process, not the merits; an appellate court interferes only where the lower court was plainly wrong
A magistrate has broad discretion to adjourn, weighing the history of the case, seriousness of the offence and the public interest
Magistrates (and tribunals) should not be named as parties or actively defend their decisions in judicial review - they must remain neutral
outcome: Appeal dismissed; the magistrate's decision to adjourn upheld as a proper exercise of discretion. The Court ruled that the practice of joining magistrates as parties to judicial review of their decisions should be discontinued. Parties to be heard on appeal costs.
osint_notes: Judicial-review/criminal-procedure ruling with significant precedent on magistrates as parties. No commercial entities, property or financial content. Crime tracking: underlying drink-driving charge.

social_headline: Appeal Court overturns malicious-prosecution win over liquor-licence charges
social_post: The Court of Appeal has reversed a malicious-prosecution award in a liquor-licence case. Dervanand Ramjattan had won at the High Court after being charged in 2014 with four breaches of the Liquor Licences Act, with the trial judge finding the police lacked reasonable cause and acted maliciously. But the Appeal Court, led by Justices Bereaux and Mohammed, found the trial judge made material errors and ignored the defence's evidence. The charging officer, Inspector Ramkelawan, had investigated for nearly six months and acted on his superior's instructions, so even if some charges lacked reasonable cause, there was no malice. The appeal was allowed and the award set aside. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Malicious prosecution requires both absence of reasonable and probable cause AND malice - sloppiness or recklessness alone is not malice
Appellate courts defer to trial judges' findings but may intervene where the judge ignored a party's case or made material errors
Acting on a superior's instructions after a thorough investigation supports an honest belief in a proper basis to charge
outcome: Appeal allowed; the trial judge's findings of no reasonable cause and malice set aside. The charging officer held to have had reasonable cause and to have acted without malice. Parties to be heard on costs.

Ashmead Ali Contracting Services sued Kaizen Trading for over $9.2 million on a running account that exchanged some $69 million in concrete, sand and aggregate from 2010 to 2019, plus disputed loans. Both sides kept poor records and tried to walk back a 2019 letter to First Citizens Bank admitting Kaizen owed $4.23 million. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC found the claim inflated and rejected a suspicious $2 million 'loan' meant to double to $4 million in months, but held Kaizen still owed a net $5,322,257.22. Kaizen's $1.6 million counterclaim was dismissed as unsupported. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Informal trading on trust without proper records leaves both sides struggling to prove their figures
An unqualified written admission of debt (e.g. a letter to the bank) is strong evidence parties cannot easily resile from
A loan that doubles the principal in months invites scrutiny and may be disbelieved absent a cogent explanation
outcome: Judgment for the claimant: Kaizen to pay $5,322,257.22 as damages for breach of contract plus $237,222.57 costs. The claimant's claimed $4 million 'loan' and $1 million truck-loss claim were rejected; the defendant's $1,645,511.67 counterclaim dismissed with $141,275.58 costs.
osint_notes: Large commercial running-account dispute naming two construction companies and their MDs, plus suppliers (Readymix) and a third-party debtor (KJS Enterprises), with multi-million-dollar figures and a bank (First Citizens). Strong commercial-network and financial OSINT value; no criminal content.

social_headline: Appeal Court quashes 50-year resentence for 1978 child murder after 46 years in custody
social_post: The Court of Appeal has quashed the resentencing of Peter Matthews, convicted in 1984 of the 1978 murder of four-year-old Roslyn Lucas. Matthews was sentenced to death, but his sentence was commuted to life imprisonment in 1994 under the Pratt and Morgan principle, and he has been in custody for some 46 years. A resentencing judge gave him 50 years in May 2025 with rehabilitation programs and probation conditions. The Appeal Court, led by Justice Maria Wilson, held that exercise was flawed and effectively imposed an original sentence that would deny both sides a proper right of appeal, so it remitted the matter to another High Court judge for fresh resentencing. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Resentencing must be conducted so as to preserve the parties' right of appeal
Constitutional relief is not normally a substitute for an appeal against conviction or sentence
Long pre-sentence custody and rehabilitation must be properly factored into a fresh sentence
outcome: Appeal allowed; the 26 May 2025 resentence (50 years) quashed and the matter remitted to the High Court for resentencing before another judge, with prison reports on the appellant's rehabilitation programs to be provided.

social_headline: Homeowners' cracked-slab claim against concrete supplier crumbles without expert proof
social_post: A Toco couple who blamed defective ready-mix concrete for their cracking, sagging floor slab have lost their case against supplier DG Readymix. Regan Ramkissoon and Shamiza Ali-Ramkissoon said loads kept too long in mixer trucks - one of which got stuck on the access road - produced weak concrete, pointing to a CARIRI core-sample report. But Justice Joan Charles struck out their technologist's report for not complying with the expert-evidence rules, and the CARIRI author who actually tested the cores was never called. With their own contractor admitting decking can fail for many reasons, the couple couldn't prove the concrete caused the damage. practical_takeaways: A claimant alleging defective goods must prove causation, usually with admissible expert evidence
Expert reports must comply with CPR Part 33; a report relying on another's untested findings can be struck out
Res ipsa loquitur will not fill an evidential gap where the damage could have many causes
outcome: Claim dismissed. Without admissible expert evidence the claimants failed to prove the concrete was defective or breached the Sale of Goods Act. Claimants ordered to pay prescribed costs on a claim value of $359,934.69.

social_headline: Injured truck driver's bid for bigger payout fails on appeal over unproven market earnings
social_post: A driver/salesman who fell off a truck while making deliveries has lost his appeal for higher damages. Rennie Bissoon was injured in 2014 and won a negligence claim against his employer, Absolute Transport (formerly K&C Logistics), receiving $100,000 general damages plus future-earnings and care awards. He appealed, arguing the judge over-discounted his income, ignored his weekend market-vending profits and wrongly refused past earnings and extra medical costs. The Court of Appeal, with Justice Dean-Armorer writing, held an appellate court only interferes where a damages award is wrong in principle or wildly off - and the trial judge wasn't. His market profits and past losses weren't properly proved or pleaded. Appeal dismissed. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: An appeal court will not disturb a damages award unless it is wrong in principle or a wholly erroneous estimate
Past loss of earnings and quantifiable medical costs are special damages that must be specifically pleaded and proved
Uncorroborated oral evidence of income (e.g. market profits) may be rejected, especially where corroborating witnesses or documents were available but not produced
outcome: Appeal dismissed; the trial judge's assessment of damages upheld. The 25% income discount, multiplier of 5, refusal of unproven market-vendor profits, unpleaded past earnings and unpleaded extra medical costs were all within the judge's proper discretion.

social_headline: Court rejects 17 ex-soldiers' $55M pension claim - can't rewrite the Defence Act
social_post: Seventeen former Defence Force members have lost a constitutional claim seeking about $55 million in retirement and pension benefits. They argued the State breached their rights by never making regulations under the Defence Act that would let them earn a monthly pension, leaving them worse off than others. Justice Frank Seepersad dismissed the claim, holding that the soldiers enlisted knowing the statutory framework - including compulsory retirement at 45, which could fall before the qualifying period for a pension. Extending pension entitlement and assuming new financial liabilities, he said, is for Parliament and the Executive, not the courts. The claim was dismissed with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Courts will not retrospectively rewrite a statutory pension framework to create entitlements Parliament did not authorise
Decisions on extending pension liability and allocating finite public resources fall to the Legislature and Executive
Entering service with knowledge of the governing rules undercuts a later claim of unfair deprivation
outcome: Claim dismissed in its entirety. The court held it could not expand State pension liability beyond the statutory framework; that is a matter for Parliament. Claimants to pay the defendant's costs.

practical_takeaways: In a workplace-illness claim the employee must prove both breach of the duty to provide a safe system of work and causation
Vague, undated medical reports without test results or prescriptions carry little weight
Refusing the employer's medical examinations and selectively visiting doctors can undermine a personal-injury claim
outcome: Claim dismissed. The claimant failed to prove UDeCOTT breached its duty of care or that the building's air quality caused his illness. Claimant ordered to pay $48,800 prescribed costs.

Two 2020 articles headlined 'Playgirl Singh' and 'Playgirl to Face US Court' accused her of fraudulently getting a $500,000 bank loan, stealing from a sick man, and facing US theft and fraud proceedings. Warner admitted in court he had no proof and was just 'providing the documentation', and the source - businessman Jorge Quintero - was never called. Republishing after being told a story is false, and refusing to apologise, are aggravating features increasing damages

social_headline: Appeal Court trims judge's orders in TTCB election and NGC audit fight
social_post: The Court of Appeal has partly reined in a High Court ruling arising from a Trinidad and Tobago Cricket Board election dispute. TTCB member Dinanath Ramnarine had challenged the 2019 executive election as non-compliant with the board's constitution. The trial judge rejected that challenge but went further, ordering the TTCB to set up a detailed five-member committee to investigate a leaked National Gas Company audit report of the board. The Appeal Court, with Justice Dean-Armorer writing, held the judge overstepped by prescribing the committee's make-up and granting relief not sought: it kept only a bare declaration that the TTCB should appoint a committee to investigate the NGC report, struck out the rest, and dismissed Ramnarine's cross-appeal on the election. He must pay 60% of the appeal costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: In judicial review a judge should generally not grant relief that was not sought by the parties
A court can declare that a body should investigate, but should not micro-manage how (e.g. dictating committee membership)
Whether a constitutional/rule provision is mandatory or directory determines if non-compliance nullifies an election
outcome: Appeal allowed in part: the trial judge's detailed orders for a five-member investigative committee and the 13 Feb 2020 consequential orders struck out, leaving only a declaration that the TTCB should appoint a committee to investigate the NGC report. Ramnarine's cross-appeal (election non-compliance) dismissed; he pays 60% of the appeal costs and all cross-appeal costs.

In 2008 the Yearwood/King family let their granddaughter Afi and her then-fiance Vernon Phipps build a home on a parcel of the family's Dades Trace land for their marriage. The couple married in 2009, but the marriage broke down in 2017 and Afi moved out - while Phipps stayed put and ignored a notice to quit. Phipps filed no defence and led no evidence, only arguing in submissions that his licence was meant to be for life. Justice Karen Reid held the arrangement was a revocable, rent-free licence that ended on the licensor's death and with the notice to quit, and ordered him to give up possession within 30 days and pay $14,000 costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A rent-free permission to occupy family land is usually a revocable licence that creates no interest in the land
A licence generally ends on the licensor's death and can be revoked by notice to quit
Arguments of an irrevocable licence or constructive trust need a pleaded case and evidence - they cannot be raised in submissions alone
outcome: Judgment for the claimants. The defendant held only a revocable licence (terminated on the licensor's death and by the 2018 notice to quit) and must deliver vacant possession of the Dades Trace lot within 30 days and pay $14,000 costs. No trespass damages awarded.
osint_notes: Family-land possession dispute with an identified Rio Claro parcel (Crown Grant Vol 235 Folio 49). Property data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: Stoute v University of Trinidad and Tobago
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago

companies.name: University of Trinidad and Tobago (UTT)
companies.ownership_notes: State university; employed the claimant as Professor in Practice and Head of the Environmental Studies Department

individuals.name: Valerie Stoute
individuals.notes: Professor in Practice and Head of Environmental Studies at UTT; placed on administrative leave during a misconduct investigation that was dropped; later retired

financial_figures.what_it_is: Breach-of-contract damages the claimant sought
Claimant's monthly base salary (2013-2019)
Ex gratia emolument, benefits and salary received on retirement
Nominal damages awarded for breach of the implied term of trust and confidence

related_litigation: Roger Carrington v University of Trinidad and Tobago CV2016-03460 (cited)
social_headline: UTT professor wins only $15k after botched misconduct probe before her retirement
social_post: A University of Trinidad and Tobago professor has largely lost her near-$1 million claim against the institution. Valerie Stoute, who headed UTT's Environmental Studies Department until the programme was discontinued, sued for breach of contract and fiduciary duty after being placed on administrative leave in 2018 over misconduct allegations that were eventually dropped. Justice Lambert Peterson held an employer owes no general fiduciary duty, and that UTT did not breach the contract by failing to renew it or raise her pay. But the way it ran the investigation - leaving her in limbo for months, ignoring its own legal advice - breached the implied term of trust and confidence. As she suffered no provable loss (she kept full pay and retired normally), she was awarded just $15,000 in nominal damages. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: An employer does not generally owe an employee a fiduciary duty; fiduciary duties arise only from specific undertakings
Every employment contract carries an implied term of mutual trust and confidence, which a heavy-handed investigation can breach
Where a breach causes no provable loss, only nominal damages (at the court's discretion) are awarded
outcome: Partial success. No fiduciary duty found; no breach in failing to renew the contract, raise pay or apply the pre-retirement policy. UTT breached the implied term of trust and confidence by the manner of its misconduct investigation, but the claimant proved no loss. Nominal damages of $15,000 awarded; costs to be decided on submissions.
osint_notes: Employment dispute naming a state university (UTT) with academic-salary and retirement figures. Institutional/employment data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

individuals.notes: Occupied the land as a permissive licensee, not adversely, so no possessory title passed to his estate

financial_figures.what_it_is: Costs of the claim awarded to the defendant (a further $14,000 awarded on the counterclaim)
