property_lease.outcome: Claim dismissed; occupation by the claimant's predecessor was permissive. Property removed from Clyde Hernandez's estate; 2017 Deed of Assent set aside; claimant restrained from entering or dealing with the land

related_litigation: Estate of Clyde Hernandez L 465 of 2016 (inventory to be amended)
social_headline: Talparo land claim fails - decades of occupation 'with permission' can't ripen into ownership
social_post: A bid to claim title to a small lot at Souria Trace, Talparo, by long occupation has failed. Ramnarine Chickoree said he and his predecessors had held the 37-perch lot for over 30 years, and sued Andrew Scott for interfering. But Justice Westmin James found the key predecessor, the late Clyde Hernandez, occupied the land only as a permissive licensee - and permission, no matter how long, does not mature into adverse possession. The claim was dismissed, the defendant's counterclaim upheld, the property removed from Hernandez's estate inventory, and a 2017 deed set aside. Chickoree was restrained from entering or dealing with the land and ordered to pay $28,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Occupation with the owner's permission, however long, cannot become adverse possession
A permissive licensee acquires no transmissible possessory title for his estate to pass on
Where a property was wrongly included in an estate, the court can amend the inventory and set aside the resulting deed
outcome: Claim dismissed. The claimant's predecessor occupied the land as a permissive licensee, so no possessory title passed; the defendant's superior claim prevailed. The lot removed from Clyde Hernandez's estate inventory, the 2017 Deed of Assent set aside, and the claimant restrained and ordered to pay $14,000 costs on the claim and $14,000 on the counterclaim.
osint_notes: Adverse-possession/estate dispute over an identified Talparo lot (Royal Grant Vol 13 Folio 563) with a set-aside deed. Property data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: Phillip v Service Commissions Department
court: High Court of Justice, Sub-Registry San Fernando, Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Andre Phillip
individuals.notes: Made a Freedom of Information request about Research Officer promotions/seniority; the department failed to respond within 30 days

related_litigation: Vijay Singh v Public Service Commission [2019] UKPC 18 (cited)
social_headline: State body broke FOI law by missing the 30-day deadline - even though it later handed over the records
social_post: A public servant has won a declaration that the Service Commissions Department broke the Freedom of Information Act. Andre Phillip asked for documents about Research Officer promotions and seniority in February 2023, but the department blew past the mandatory 30-day deadline, only producing the records in stages months later - after he had filed judicial review. Justice Robin Mohammed held that belated compliance doesn't erase the breach: 'access delayed is access denied'. The orders to compel disclosure were academic since the records were handed over, but the breach declaration stood, and the State was ordered to pay his costs - though the judge trimmed the bill, refusing to certify Senior Counsel for what was a well-settled point. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A public authority has an absolute duty to decide a FOI request within 30 days; the breach occurs on the 31st day
Later voluntary disclosure does not cure or moot the statutory breach - a declaration still vindicates the applicant
Costs against the State for FOI delay must be proportionate; Senior Counsel may not be certified where the point is well-settled
outcome: Declaration that the Service Commissions Department breached section 15 of the FOIA by missing the 30-day deadline. Mandamus and remaining declarations dismissed as academic (records since provided). Costs awarded to the claimant, certified for one Band D advocate and one instructing attorney only - no Senior Counsel.
osint_notes: Freedom-of-Information/administrative-law matter against a state body. No commercial entities, property or financial figures; no criminal content. Notable as a precedent on FOI delay and proportionate costs.

social_headline: Charuma family land stays with the estate as relative's adverse-possession claim fails
social_post: A family fight over an acre of land in Charuma has gone to the estate of the late Eurina Schullere. Her beneficiaries sued relative Gerard James, whose grandmother (the deceased's daughter) had been allowed to occupy the land. James claimed he'd held it long enough to own it by adverse possession. Justice Westmin James found the occupation was permissive and never became adverse, so no possessory title arose. James was ordered to deliver up vacant possession within three months, restrained from harassing the claimants, and ordered to pay mesne profits of $500 a month (about $22,700 to date and continuing) plus $28,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Occupation that starts with family permission does not ripen into adverse possession without a clear change to adverse intention
An adverse possessor must show exclusive, undisturbed possession with intent to exclude the true owner for the limitation period
A successful owner can recover mesne profits for the period of wrongful occupation after a notice to quit
outcome: Judgment for the claimants. The defendant's adverse-possession counterclaim failed (occupation was permissive). He must deliver up vacant possession within 3 months, is restrained from interfering with or harassing the claimants, and must pay mesne profits ($22,709.68 plus $500/month ongoing) and costs of $14,000 on the claim and $14,000 on the counterclaim.
osint_notes: Family estate/adverse-possession dispute over an identified Charuma parcel (Crown Grant Vol 196 Folio 427) with mesne-profit and costs figures. Property data of OSINT value; no criminal content.

title: Skeete v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Curtis Skeete
individuals.notes: Retired Police Sergeant (regimental #12055); 35 years' service; unpaid pension and gratuity for over five years

related_litigation: Chandresh Sharma v AG Civil Appeal No. 52 of 2005 (binding - salary/benefits are property under s.4(a))
Samuel Friday v AG CV2021-02843 (materially similar facts)
social_headline: Retired cop's pension unpaid for 5 years - court lets his constitutional claim proceed
social_post: A retired police sergeant who served 35 years has been allowed to pursue a constitutional claim against the State for never paying his pension and gratuity. Curtis Skeete retired in September 2020, but despite about 20 phone calls and 10 visits to the Police Administration Building - on one of which he had to help find his own misplaced increment certificate - nothing was paid. The State tried to strike the claim out as a mere employment matter dressed up as a constitutional one. Justice Robin Mohammed refused: binding authority holds that earned pension and gratuity are 'property' protected by the Constitution, and the prolonged, unexplained delay (now over five years) was exceptional. The strike-out was dismissed, the State ordered to pay costs and to file its defence within 28 days. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Earned pension and gratuity are property protected by section 4(a) of the Constitution, not merely contractual entitlements
Constitutional redress is reserved for exceptional cases, but prolonged, unexplained State delay can qualify
Where no adequate parallel remedy genuinely exists, the constitutional jurisdiction may be properly invoked
outcome: The State's application to strike out dismissed. The court held the constitutional jurisdiction was properly invoked (pension/gratuity are property under s.4(a); no adequate alternative remedy; the years-long unexplained delay was exceptional). State to pay the costs of the application and file its evidence in answer within 28 days; CMC fixed for 25 June 2026.
osint_notes: Constitutional/pension claim by a retired police officer against the State. No commercial entities, property or quantified figures; no criminal content. Part of a pattern of police-pension-delay litigation.

title: Camacho Teran v The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago
defendants: The Attorney General of Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Hober Luis Camacho Teran
individuals.notes: Venezuelan/Colombian national and UNHCR asylum seeker; detained June 2019 after entering by boat; challenged a 2022 deportation order

financial_figures.what_it_is: Amount the claimant said he paid a boat captain for passage to Trinidad

related_litigation: Jesus Alexander Rodriguez Martinez v The Chief Immigration Officer [2022] UKPC 29 (cited)
social_headline: Venezuelan asylum seeker's deportation challenge fails - UNHCR card is not law here
social_post: A Venezuelan and Colombian national has lost his constitutional challenge to his deportation from Trinidad and Tobago. Hober Luis Camacho Teran entered illegally by boat in June 2019 amid a surge of Venezuelan arrivals, was briefly detained, and later served with a 2022 deportation order. He argued his arrest was unlawful and that the State should honour the 1951 Refugee Convention, a 2014 draft national refugee policy and his pending UNHCR application. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC dismissed the claim: his entry was unlawful, the Minister had a broad statutory power to deport, the Refugee Convention is not part of T&T law, the draft policy was never implemented, and a UNHCR card confers no protection from the Immigration Act. The claim was dismissed with costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: International treaties like the 1951 Refugee Convention create no enforceable rights unless incorporated into domestic law
A UNHCR asylum-seeker card does not confer legal status or protect against deportation under the Immigration Act
A draft, unimplemented government policy and general political statements do not create an enforceable legitimate expectation
outcome: Claim dismissed. The detention was lawful under the Immigration Act and the deportation order a valid exercise of ministerial power; no enforceable legitimate expectation or treaty right protected the claimant, and the failure to inform him of his right to counsel caused no material prejudice. Claimant ordered to pay the State's costs.
osint_notes: Immigration/asylum constitutional challenge by a Venezuelan/Colombian national, touching on the 2014 refugee policy, UNHCR and Venezuelan migration figures. No commercial entities or property; sensitive migration topic handled as public-record fact. Limited OSINT value.

related_litigation: National Insurance Appeals Tribunal decision of 3 August 2023 (held it had no jurisdiction - issue was one of law)
banks_financial_institutions: The National Insurance Board of Trinidad and Tobago (NIB)
social_headline: Retired IT man loses NIS pension fight - he was a consultant, not an employee
social_post: A retired engineer has lost his appeal to have years of work counted toward a National Insurance retirement pension. Richard Chang Kit did IT work for the Tobago law firm Gift & Co. on a $1,250 monthly retainer, and the firm paid NIS for him. But when he applied for a pension, the NIB found his role was a 'contract for service' - an independent consultant, not an employee. Justice Avason Quinlan-Williams, rehearing the appeal, agreed: he used his own equipment, worked only a few days a month, was free to work for others to 'sustain himself', and got no vacation or sick leave. So he qualifies only for a $63,445 Retirement Grant, not a pension. The judge urged the NIB to give detailed reasons earlier in such cases. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Whether someone is an 'employee' under a contract of service is decided by weighing many factors, not one control test
Working for multiple clients, using your own tools and receiving no benefits point toward independent-contractor status
Registering for and paying NIS creates only a presumption of employment, not an entitlement to benefits
outcome: Appeal dismissed. On a rehearing, the court found the claimant worked under a contract for service (as an IT consultant), not a contract of service, so the disputed contributions could not count toward a pension. He is entitled only to a Retirement Grant of $63,445.41. The court recommended the NIB give detailed reasons at the preliminary-determination stage.
osint_notes: National Insurance/employment-status appeal naming the NIB and a Tobago law firm (Gift & Co., partner Jocelyn Gift), with contribution and grant figures. Useful on employee-vs-contractor classification; no criminal content.

title: Sookoo v The Chief Personnel Officer
court: High Court of Justice, Trinidad and Tobago

individuals.name: Marsha Sookoo
individuals.notes: Forester I in the Forestry Division, Ministry of Agriculture; sought FOI documents about why her University of Idaho degree was held not equivalent to a Forestry degree for promotion to Assistant Conservator of Forests

related_litigation: Vijay Singh v Ministry of Agriculture / PSC / AG CV2016-02327 and CA S090/2017 (a colleague's similar challenge, partly successful)
social_headline: Forester wins FOI fight to see why her US degree was rejected for promotion
social_post: A government forester has largely won a Freedom of Information battle against the Chief Personnel Officer. Marsha Sookoo earned a Resource, Recreation and Tourism degree from the University of Idaho on a scholarship, but was repeatedly told it wasn't equivalent to a Forestry degree needed to become Assistant Conservator of Forests. She used FOI to seek the comparative analysis, the evaluators' details and the equivalency guidelines, and was refused. Justice Avason Quinlan-Williams held the refusals breached the FOIA: she's entitled to the assessment report (with officers' identities redacted), the job titles of the evaluators (but not their names), and the equivalency guidelines, which weren't truly exempt. The CPO was ordered to hand them over within 14 days and pay costs fit for senior counsel. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The FOIA has a strong bias toward disclosure; a public authority claiming an exemption must justify it with evidence
Deliberative internal documents can still be disclosed with personal identities redacted under s.16(2)
Job titles of officials may be disclosable in the public interest even where their names are not
outcome: Judgment largely for the claimant. The CPO's refusals under the FOIA held illegal. She is entitled to the comparative-analysis Assessment Report (redacted to protect officers' identities), the job titles/designations (not names) of the evaluators, and the equivalency guidelines. Mandamus to produce them within 14 days; costs to the claimant fit for senior and junior counsel.
osint_notes: Freedom-of-Information/public-service-promotion matter naming the Chief Personnel Officer and the Forestry Division of the Ministry of Agriculture, concerning a University of Idaho degree-equivalency dispute. No commercial entities, property or figures; no criminal content.

title: The State v Lewis, Christopher
court: High Court of Justice, Criminal Division, Port of Spain

individuals.name: Jeffrey Bonaparte
individuals.notes: Shot multiple times on 29 Dec 2023; identified the accused but the recognition was held unreliable under Turnbull

individuals.name: Dane Baker
individuals.notes: Recognition witness; admitted fabricating part of his evidence and that he did not see the accused's face during the shooting

individuals.name: Michael Baggan
individuals.notes: Recognition witness; uncertainty about his position and the quality of his observation

criminal_individuals.name: Christopher Lewis
offences: shooting with intent to do grievous bodily harm
criminal_individuals.notes: Aka 'Rockback'. Charged over the 29 Dec 2023 shooting of Jeffrey Bonaparte. Found Not Guilty on all four counts because the recognition evidence of three witnesses was held insufficiently reliable under R v Turnbull, with no firearm, forensic or CCTV evidence to support it.

related_litigation: A matter involving Tristan De Silva, in which the accused was a State witness (alleged motive for fabrication)
social_headline: 'Rockback' acquitted of attempted murder as judge finds split-second IDs unreliable
social_post: A man known as 'Rockback' has been acquitted of attempting to murder Jeffrey Bonaparte, who was shot multiple times in Port of Spain in December 2023. Three witnesses who all knew Christopher Lewis said he was the gunman, but Justice Nalini Singh applied the Turnbull caution on identification and found their evidence too weak to be sure. Bonaparte's view lasted only 'a couple of seconds' at night while he was falling under gunfire; one witness admitted fabricating part of his account and conceded he never saw the shooter's face; the third gave conflicting evidence about where he even was. With no gun recovered, no forensics and no CCTV, the judge was left with reasonable doubt. Lewis was found Not Guilty on all four counts. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Even recognition of a known person requires the Turnbull caution; honest witnesses can still be mistaken
A brief, stressful observation during gunfire is a serious weakness in identification evidence
Multiple weak identifications do not become proof beyond reasonable doubt merely by accumulation, especially without forensic or CCTV support
outcome: Not Guilty on all four counts (attempted murder, shooting with intent, possession of firearm and ammunition). The recognition evidence of the three witnesses was held unreliable under Turnbull and unsupported by any firearm, forensic or CCTV evidence; identity not proved to the criminal standard.
osint_notes: Firearms/attempted-murder prosecution ending in acquittal on identification grounds. No commercial entities, property or financial content. Tracked for crime intelligence (firearms, shooting, acquittal).

social_headline: Massy wins $947k debt judgment as Jusamco's 'can't admit or deny' defence is struck out
social_post: Massy Machinery has won a near-million-dollar debt judgment against Jusamco Pavers, part of the Junior Sammy Group. Massy sold the company equipment and maintenance services between 2017 and 2021 and said it was owed $946,962.68. Jusamco's defence mostly said it could neither admit nor deny the invoices and put Massy to proof. Justice Margaret Mohammed held that, having acknowledged most of the debt in earlier correspondence and offered to pay it off in instalments, the company's 'can't admit or deny' stance was a bare denial that breached the pleading rules. She struck out the defence (except for older, statute-barred 2015-2016 invoices Massy dropped) and entered judgment for $946,962.68 plus interest and over $73,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A defence of 'can neither admit nor deny' is a bare denial that breaches CPR 10.5 unless reasons and search efforts are given
Acknowledging a debt and proposing instalment payments undermines a later attempt to dispute it
Without-prejudice settlement correspondence can be admitted to explain alleged delay in pursuing a debt
outcome: Defence struck out (save for statute-barred 2015-2016 invoices the claimant abandoned). Judgment for the claimant for $946,962.68 plus $21,394.89 interest, costs of the application, and $73,515.54 prescribed costs.

DCM had missed the deadline to file a defence to Howida's counterclaim, which would normally mean it was deemed to admit the counterclaim. social_headline: Contractor loses appeal over a $716k income-tax assessment for 2013
social_post: A New Grant building contractor has lost his appeal against a large income-tax bill. Jagdeo Jankie challenged the Board of Inland Revenue's assessment of additional income tax for 2013, arguing the BIR's communications were too unclear to tell him how the figures were worked out. The Tax Appeal Board dismissed his application, and the Court of Appeal - with Justice Mark Mohammed writing - agreed: although the BIR's figures could have been presented more tidily, the tables, schedules and tax objection report gave him enough detail to understand the roughly $716,000 in taxes, interest and levy claimed. Appeal courts are slow to overturn the Tax Appeal Board's findings unless plainly wrong

social_headline: Both sides lose their applications in Arima Corporation pay dispute
social_post: A procedural standoff between Arnold Pinto and the Arima Borough Corporation has ended with neither side getting what it wanted. Pinto, who the Corporation says has not reported to duty since August 2018, sued over his pay; the Corporation counterclaimed that paying him for services not rendered would be unjust enrichment. Pinto filed his defence to that counterclaim late and sought relief from sanctions; the Corporation asked the court to treat the counterclaim as admitted. Justice Karen Reid dismissed both applications and struck out the late defence, finding the unjust-enrichment counterclaim depended on the outcome of the main claim and could only be decided at trial. Each side bears its own costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A counterclaim that merely anticipates the outcome of the main claim may disclose no independent cause of action
Relief from sanctions can be refused even where the opposing 'deem admitted' application also fails
Unjust-enrichment claims contingent on a trial finding are dealt with after, not before, trial
outcome: Both applications dismissed: the claimant's relief-from-sanctions/extension application refused (and his late defence to counterclaim struck out), and the defendant's application to deem the counterclaim admitted also dismissed. Each party to bear its own costs.
osint_notes: Employment/procedural dispute against a municipal corporation. No commercial entities beyond the public body, no property or financial figures, no criminal content.

social_headline: State pays $225k for arresting a man over a charge thrown out a decade earlier
social_post: A Chaguanas mechanic and farmer has won $225,000 from the State after being arrested, detained and prosecuted in 2015 on a criminal charge that a Magistrate had already dismissed back in 2005. Suruj Samaroo brought a constitutional claim; the State filed no evidence, so his account stood uncontradicted. Justice Jacqueline Wilson KC found his rights to liberty, protection of the law and a fair hearing were breached, and that the systemic failures - prosecuting him on a dead charge despite his repeated protests, with no explanation from the State - made the violation grave. She awarded $200,000 compensatory and $25,000 vindicatory damages, plus interest and costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Re-prosecuting someone on a charge already dismissed can breach constitutional rights to liberty and protection of the law
Where the State files no evidence, the claimant's uncontradicted affidavit is accepted
Vindicatory damages may be added where compensation alone does not mark the gravity of a rights breach
outcome: Claim succeeds. Declared that the claimant's rights under ss.4(a), 4(b), 5(2)(c)(ii) and 5(2)(h) were contravened by his 2015 arrest and prosecution on a 2005-dismissed charge. title: The State v Als, Joseph

Pleaded guilty to manslaughter on the basis of diminished responsibility. Sentenced by Justice Nalini Singh to 8 years 8 months' imprisonment (16-year starting point reduced to 13 for mitigation, then a one-third guilty-plea discount), with the full pre-trial custody (~4 years 3 months) deducted; bonded $5,000 to keep the peace for 3 years on release.

social_headline: La Horquetta man jailed for stabbing nephew to death; murder reduced to manslaughter
social_post: Joseph Als, known as 'Joe', has been sentenced for killing his nephew Marvin Hosten at La Horquetta in May 2020. On the morning of the stabbing his sister noticed him acting strangely with a knife wrapped in a bandana and warned her son to be careful; soon after she left for work, the two had an altercation in the yard and Als stabbed Hosten multiple times. Charged with murder, Als pleaded guilty to manslaughter on the basis of diminished responsibility. Justice Nalini Singh set a 16-year starting point, reduced it to 13 years for his mental condition, applied a one-third plea discount to 8 years 8 months, and deducted his time on remand. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A murder charge can be reduced to manslaughter where diminished responsibility is established by a plea agreement
Sentencing starts from a notional starting point, adjusted for mitigation, then a guilty-plea discount, then credit for time served
Courts may add a peace bond and probation conditions (psychiatric follow-up, no alcohol/drugs) on release
outcome: Pleaded guilty to manslaughter (diminished responsibility) on a murder indictment. Sentenced to 8 years 8 months' imprisonment (16-year starting point reduced to 13 for mitigation, then a one-third plea discount), with full pre-trial custody (~4 years 3 months) deducted; bonded $5,000 to keep the peace for 3 years on release with probation/psychiatric conditions.
osint_notes: Domestic manslaughter (knife) sentencing. No commercial entities or property; tracked for crime intelligence (homicide reduced to manslaughter, diminished responsibility).

social_headline: State punished for clawing back officer's allowance while sparing a colleague
social_post: Justice Westmin James has found the State breached the constitutional rights of public officer Troy Singh to protection of the law and equality of treatment. After Singh was seconded from the Prison Service to Customs and Excise, the State stopped his pensionable allowance, then a decade later branded the payments overpayments and demanded $122,108 back — even though a colleague in identical circumstances, Stephen Jack, had already won constitutional relief over the very same allowance. The court set aside the repayment demand and awarded Singh $172,154 in total damages ($97,154 compensatory, $25,000 aggravated, $50,000 vindicatory), and ordered his pension and benefits recalculated. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: The State must treat materially similar public officers consistently; differential treatment can breach the right to equality
A long-delayed demand to recharacterize past payments as overpayments can be struck down as unconstitutional
Aggravated and vindicatory damages may be added to compensation where State conduct is high-handed
outcome: Constitutional motion succeeded. Repayment demand of $122,108 set aside as null and void; claimant awarded $172,154 total damages ($97,154 compensatory, $25,000 aggravated, $50,000 vindicatory) plus interest; pension, gratuity and retirement benefits to be recalculated within 90 days; costs to claimant; 42-day stay.
osint_notes: Constitutional/equality ruling against the State with a named comparator (Stephen Jack). Tracks government liability over public-service pension allowances; no private commercial entities.

social_post: Justice Kevin Ramcharan has struck out a constitutional claim brought by Carla Trotman Parks, a former data clerk with the Tobago House of Assembly, over how her salary scale, gratuity and leave entitlements were calculated during years of short-term contracts. The court held that disputes about workers' terms and conditions generally belong before the Industrial Court as a trade dispute, and that invoking the constitutional jurisdiction was a misuse of process. But, noting the THA never gave Parks the courtesy of an official response over many years, the judge departed from the usual rule and made no order as to costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Workplace terms-and-conditions disputes generally belong in the Industrial Court, not a constitutional motion
Using the constitutional jurisdiction for what is really a trade dispute can be struck out as a misuse of process
A court may decline to award costs against a struck-out claimant where the public body behaved poorly
outcome: Claim struck out as a matter that should have been brought before the Industrial Court as a trade dispute; no order as to costs (court critical of the THA's failure to respond to the claimant over many years).

social_headline: Ex-constable wins after 20-year fight: court says firing breached his rights
social_post: Nearly two decades after he was dismissed from the Trinidad and Tobago Police Service, former constable Virgil Wharton has won his judicial review. He had been fired by the Police Service Commission in 2004 over a larceny charge that a magistrate had already dismissed without any conviction, and the Public Service Appeal Board upheld the dismissal. Justice Robin Mohammed declared that dismissing him without a proper hearing breached his constitutional rights to the protection of the law and equality before the law, and that the Appeal Board erred in affirming it. Compensation — including lost salary and damages for distress — was referred to a Master for assessment. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A public officer must be given a fair hearing before dismissal; skipping mandatory procedures can be unconstitutional
A dismissed criminal charge (no conviction) is a weak foundation for disciplinary dismissal
Where rights are breached, compensation can include lost salary plus damages for distress, assessed by a Master
outcome: Judicial review succeeded. Declarations granted that the PSC's dismissal without a fair hearing and without following s.129(6) breached the claimant's rights under s.4(b) of the Constitution and that the PSAB erred in affirming it; compensation referred to a Master (lost salary/allowances plus distress damages) unless agreed within 60 days; defendant to pay costs.
osint_notes: Long-running public-service/police dismissal judicial review. No commercial entities or banks; criminal charge (larceny) was dismissed without conviction.

social_headline: Manzanilla landowners win trespass case; squatter ordered off and to pay $30,000
social_post: After an eight-year battle, Dexter and Michelle Alexis have won their trespass claim over a parcel of land at Manzanilla. Justice Robin Mohammed found that Jeffrey James had trespassed on their land, ordered him to give up possession and remove the poles he had erected, and granted an injunction barring him from blocking the couple's access. He was ordered to pay $30,000 in damages plus interest and $14,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A landowner who proves title can recover possession, damages and an injunction against a trespasser
Pre-judgment interest can add up over a long-running case (here nearly 8 years)
Courts can order a trespasser to remove structures (e.g. poles/fencing) erected on the land
outcome: Judgment for the claimants. Defendant found to have trespassed; ordered to deliver up possession, remove poles, and restrained by injunction; awarded $30,000 damages plus $5,773.97 interest and $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Private land-trespass/possession dispute (Manzanilla). No companies, banks or criminal element.

social_headline: Tobago siblings' land feud: brother kept off sister's land except his own house lot
social_post: A land dispute between an elderly brother and sister at Signal Hill, Tobago has gone largely against the brother. Samuel McLetchie claimed ownership of the family parcel, but Justice Robin Mohammed dismissed his claim and gave judgment to his sister, Stella, the registered owner, on her counterclaim. The court declared Samuel had no right to the land except the single lot on which his own dwelling house stands, granted a permanent injunction keeping him off the rest of the parcel, and ordered him to pay $28,000 in costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Long occupation of family land does not override a registered owner's title
A court can recognise a limited right to the lot under one's home while denying any claim to the wider parcel
Losing both a claim and a counterclaim can mean paying two sets of prescribed costs
outcome: Claim dismissed; judgment for the defendant on her counterclaim. Declaration that the claimant has no entitlement to the Signal Hill parcel except the single lot on which his dwelling house stands; permanent injunction in the defendant's favour; claimant to pay $14,000 + $14,000 costs.
osint_notes: Private sibling land-ownership dispute (Signal Hill, Tobago). No companies, banks or criminal element.

title: Telecommunications Services of Trinidad and Tobago v Telecommunications Authority of Trinidad and Tobago
court: Court of Appeal

companies.name: Telecommunications Services of Trinidad and Tobago Limited (TSTT)
companies.ownership_notes: National telecommunications provider; challenged the regulator's 2021 interconnection Determination

companies.name: Telecommunications Authority of Trinidad and Tobago (TATT)
companies.ownership_notes: Regulator established under the Telecommunications Act Ch. 47:21; published Determination 2021/01 (16 March 2021) and a 2021 Interconnection Benchmarking Study

related_litigation: High Court Claim No. CV2021-02135 (judicial review of Determination 2021/01)
social_headline: Appeal Court quashes telecoms regulator's 2021 interconnection ruling, orders consultation
social_post: The Court of Appeal has sided with TSTT in its battle with the telecommunications regulator, TATT. At issue was TATT's Determination 2021/01, published in March 2021 alongside an interconnection benchmarking study, which set price-regulation terms for the sector. Delivering the court's judgment, Justice of Appeal Peter Rajkumar (with Henderson and Rahim JJA agreeing) granted certiorari quashing the Determination and ordered TATT, by mandamus, to publish the benchmarking study and a draft determination for proper consultation with reasonable timelines. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: A regulator must follow its own published consultation procedures before making binding determinations
Courts can quash a regulatory determination (certiorari) and compel a fresh, consultative process (mandamus)
Procedural fairness applies even to expert economic/price regulation of the telecoms sector
outcome: Appeal allowed. Order of certiorari quashing TATT's Determination 2021/01; order of mandamus compelling TATT to publish the 2021 Interconnection Benchmarking Study and a draft determination for consultation in accordance with its published procedures and reasonable timelines.
osint_notes: Significant Court of Appeal regulatory ruling involving the national telecoms provider (TSTT) and the regulator (TATT). Relevant for sector/commercial intelligence; no banks or criminal element.

social_headline: Couple win 6 acres at Las Lomas through adverse possession after decades of occupation
social_post: Leroy and Sumintra Judy Antoine have won title to roughly six acres of agricultural land at Las Lomas through adverse possession. Justice Karen Reid found that after renting the land between 1977 and 1980, the couple stayed on it rent-free for decades after the owner abandoned it, in undisturbed and exclusive occupation for well over the 16 years the law requires. The court gave judgment for the Antoines against the estate of the late Irma Agatha Brown and Desmond Phillip, dismissed the estate's counterclaim, and awarded the couple costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Sixteen years of undisturbed, exclusive, continuous occupation can defeat a paper owner's title
A tenancy that ends can ripen into adverse possession once rent stops and the owner abandons the land
A power of attorney from the paper owner does not defeat an established adverse-possession claim
outcome: Judgment for the claimants. Declaration that they were in undisturbed, exclusive and continuous occupation of the ~6-acre disputed lands for more than 16 years (adverse possession); first defendant's counterclaim dismissed; defendants to pay the claimants' costs ($14,000 claim + $14,000 counterclaim).
osint_notes: Private adverse-possession land dispute (Las Lomas agricultural land). No companies, banks or criminal element.

social_headline: Man who built family home annex wins 70% equity after eviction
social_post: A family property dispute has ended with Justice Devindra Rampersad declaring that Llewellyn Jackson, who built and paid for an annex on the family property before being evicted in April 2021, holds an equity worth 70% of that annex's value. The court directed the parties to agree a value (or have the annex valued as at April 2021), ordered the second defendant to pay him that 70% share, and awarded $20,000 nominal damages for trespass plus costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Money spent building on family land can create an equitable interest enforceable in court
Courts can quantify that interest as a percentage of the structure's value rather than ordering possession
Eviction of someone holding such an equity can attract nominal trespass damages
outcome: Claimant declared to have an equity coupled with a 70% share in the value of the annex; second defendant to pay 70% of the annex's value (agreed or valued as at April 2021); $20,000 nominal trespass damages and prescribed costs awarded to the claimant; liberty to apply.
osint_notes: Private family property/proprietary-equity dispute. No companies, banks or criminal element.

social_headline: Court lets claimants add fresh evidence in constitutional case against the State
social_post: In a procedural ruling in long-running constitutional claims against the State, Justice Karen Reid has partly granted an application by Kendelle Khan and Lyndon James to lead fresh evidence. The court allowed them to rely on affidavits and certified cross-examination transcripts of two witnesses drawn from a separate matter, Melina Charles v Attorney General, and set a timetable for supplemental affidavits and submissions ahead of the substantive hearing. Each side was ordered to bear its own costs of the application. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Fresh evidence may be admitted late where it is cogent and material to the case as a whole
Evidence given in a related matter can sometimes be imported into another with leave of the court
Procedural applications of this kind often end with each party bearing its own costs
outcome: Interlocutory ruling: application to lead fresh evidence succeeds in part. Claimants permitted to rely on specified affidavits and certified cross-examination transcripts from CV2020-00510 (Melina Charles v AG), with a timetable for further affidavits and submissions; each party to bear its own costs of the application.
osint_notes: Procedural/evidential ruling in consolidated constitutional claims against the State. No commercial entities, banks or named criminal conduct in this ruling.

court: High Court of Justice
judges: The Hon. Mr. Justice Devindra Rampersad

companies.ownership_notes: Insurer joined as co-defendant; the applicant's earlier action had been dismissed and costs never satisfied

individuals.name: Christiana Mc Comie
individuals.notes: Sought to revive litigation after her earlier action was dismissed; application to extend time/proceed refused for delay, lack of cogent evidence and prejudice to the respondents (files closed and destroyed)

related_litigation: Earlier (first) action by the applicant arising from the same matter, dismissed with costs unsatisfied
social_headline: Court refuses to revive long-stalled claim; insurer and defendants get finality
social_post: Justice Devindra Rampersad has refused Christiana Mc Comie's bid to revive litigation after her earlier action was dismissed, naming Guardian General Insurance Limited among the co-defendants. Weighing the long delay, the absence of any cogent professional evidence explaining the conduct of the case, and the severe prejudice to the respondents — whose files had been closed and destroyed and whose witnesses could not reliably recall events nearly a decade old — the court held there was no adequate foundation for relief. The judge acknowledged the human cost but dismissed the application, by consent with no order as to costs. Full judgment at ttlawcourts.org.
practical_takeaways: Applications to extend time or revive a dismissed claim require cogent evidence and promptness
Severe prejudice to the other side — destroyed files, faded memories — can be decisive against reopening
A litigant failed by their attorney may have other avenues of redress but still cannot reopen a stale claim without foundation
outcome: Application (filed 6 May 2024) to revive/extend time dismissed. Court found undue delay, no cogent evidence on the conduct of the earlier litigation, and severe irreparable prejudice to the respondents; by consent, no order as to costs.
osint_notes: Procedural ruling refusing to revive a stale claim; names insurer Guardian General Insurance Limited as co-defendant. Relevant for insurer/litigation tracking; no criminal element.
